theme: holding a conquered FREE REPUBLIC long accustomed to self-rule
problem: You have overthrown a free republic and now face the choice of dismantling its remaining civic assemblies. If you abolish them outright, you may provoke a unified uprising among the proud citizenry, but if you leave them intact, they become natural rallying points for exiled senators to coordinate a counter-revolution from abroad.
facets: The republican spirit is not broken by occupying armies alone; it feeds on symbols, shared memory, and the lingering hope that collective deliberation can be restored.
The exiled senators are currently divided by factional rivalries, but a common enemy and an accessible political structure could unite them faster than any diplomatic appeal.
The neighboring kingdoms, though currently neutral, are watching for a pretext to intervene—either to restore the republic or to extract concessions from you under the guise of mediation.

angles.directive: Preserve the assemblies in physical form but drain them of political meaning by transforming them into ceremonial bodies that ratify your decrees—give the citizens the theater of self-rule while converting their institutions into instruments of your legitimacy, forcing exiled senators to choose between supporting a hollowed republic or appearing to oppose their own people's 'consent.'

Select a faction among the exiled senators that is most vulnerable to material temptation and secretly offer them lucrative offices and titles to return and participate in a 'reformed' senate under your protection, splitting the exile community between those who negotiate and those who refuse, thereby delaying unified action while you consolidate.

Privately approach a neighboring kingdom and offer a lucrative trade pact or territorial concession in exchange for them denying refuge to the exiled senators, effectively outsourcing the containment of your political enemies to a foreign power whose self-interest will keep them committed to your survival, even as your own troops remain focused on garrison duty.

Abolish the assemblies immediately and accept the short-term insurgency that follows, but deliberately concentrate your reprisals on the ringleaders while sparing the broader populace, calculating that the brief but sharp pain of suppression will exhaust republican fervor faster than a prolonged low-intensity contest of legitimacy that keeps hope alive for years.

threads: I will rebrand the popular assemblies as “Imperial Civic Tribunals,” retaining their meeting halls, schedules, and elected delegates but replacing their legislative agenda with a single ceremonial function: publicly acclaiming my edicts and awarding symbolic honors to loyal citizens. This bet is that the exiled senator Cassius, the most ambitious and image-conscious of the republican diehards, will refuse to endorse a hollowed institution and instead denounce the Tribunals as a farce, a reaction driven by his fear that participation would permanently taint his personal dignitas in the eyes of the purist exile faction. This response is probable only if Cassius calculates that his domestic prestige among the assemblies’ delegates is already too eroded by their collaboration to be worth salvaging.

I will dispatch envoys to Senator Vorenus, whose family’s confiscated latifundia and shipping concessions leave him uniquely susceptible to restoration, with a sealed offer of the newly created office of “Prefect of Civic Harmony” and a lifetime seat in a consultative council that carries the trappings of senatorial dignity but no legislative veto. I am betting that Vorenus’s public acceptance—driven by his pathological terror of dying in obscurity and penniless exile—will provoke the intransigent hardliner Cassian into denouncing him as a collaborator so viciously that it fractures the exile community’s moral authority before they can agree on a united front. This hinges entirely on Cassian’s vanity overriding his strategic patience, as he views Vorenus not merely as a rival but as a personal insult to the martyrdom he has already pre-written for himself.

I will dispatch an envoy to the court of King Aldric of Veridia, offering a permanent 15% share of our northern silver mines and a 99-year lease on the port of Calen in exchange for a royal decree that classifies the exiled senators as common criminals subject to immediate arrest within his borders. This bet hinges on Aldric’s venal calculus that a guaranteed stream of hard revenue and a warm-water port outweigh the uncertain gratitude of a fractured senate-in-exile, making his compliance the most probable reaction so long as his treasury remains depleted from last season’s failed harvest.

I will dissolve all assemblies by dawn edict, publicly executing the identified ringleaders while offering a 48-hour amnesty to any citizen who surrenders their assembly token to the legionaries. The exiled senator Cassius, fearing this clemency will co-opt his urban base, will likely race back with his household guard to seize command of a premature insurrection before his popular relevance evaporates. This hinges entirely on Cassius believing his personal authority alone can ignite the streets without waiting for the neighboring kingdom’s promised legions.

judge.note: Chains too long; psychological predictions unfalsifiable; fantasy-reach dominates.

theme: SACRIFICING an innocent relative to bind a powerful Gu or escape annihilation
problem: Your innocent cousin is the ideal sacrifice to fully refine the Blood Skull Gu, instantly turning you from prey into predator against the ambushing rival clan – but rivals might be baiting you to reveal this forbidden refinement, and a shadowy benefactor hints that your cousin's unique physique is the true target. Sacrifice her for guaranteed power now, or gamble by preserving her to flush out hidden schemes, knowing annihilation looms?
facets: The rival clan's ambush may be a feint to force you to expend your trump card, leaving you vulnerable for their real objective.
The shadowy benefactor's teleportation array might be a trap to kidnap your cousin, or a genuine escape route.
Sacrificing the cousin will stain your soul, potentially creating a heart demon that impedes future breakthroughs.

angles.directive: Refine the Gu, then immediately launch a preemptive slaughter of the rival clan's ambush force, using their blood to further fuel the Gu's slaughter path, deterring all enemies.
Fake the sacrifice ritual to buy time, then use the mysterious array to teleport yourself and your cousin away, forcing the rival to reveal their hand while you escape the immediate crisis.

Secretly approach the rival clan under guise of surrender, offering your cousin as a tribute, while planting a tracking Gu on her – then join forces with the rival's internal faction to topple their schemer.

Instead of sacrificing your cousin, use her pure yin physique to cultivate a mutual life-bound Gu that links your souls – if you perish, she gains your cultivation; if she perishes, you gain hers. Reveal this to the rival, turning her into a poison pill they dare not harm.

threads: I will refine the Blood Skull Gu using my cousin as the catalyst, then immediately launch a preemptive slaughter of the rival clan's ambush force, channeling their freshly spilled blood back into the Gu to trigger its Slaughter Path resonance and amplify its nascent killing intent. This move bets that **Elder Ma Rin**, the rival clan's spymaster observing from concealment, will interpret the brazen counter-slaughter not as a trap sprung but as a desperate, soul-stained berserker burning his last trump card. She is most likely to respond by committing her hidden reserve squad to pin me down, because her entire operational doctrine treats emotional recklessness as the final, exploitable stage of prey. The contingency is that her assessment framework prioritizes psychological profiling over strategic anomaly detection; if she instead reads the act as a calculated provocation, she will withdraw and leave the true target—the cousin's physique—uncontested.

I will stage a counterfeit Blood Skull refinement using a swine’s-blood substitute in a traced ritual circle, then trigger the shadowy benefactor’s teleportation array the instant Elder Shen’s spirit sense locks onto the flare, extracting my cousin and myself before the final sacrificial stroke. The bet is that Shen, witnessing the crimson flash and our sudden absence, will interpret it as a successful Gu awakening and will immediately commit his hidden Qilin-Suppressing Spear formation to seal the mountain pass, believing he must contain a cornered predator before the backlash wanes. This is his likeliest response because his clan’s entire ambush doctrine treats the Blood Skull Gu’s instant power spike as the sole existential trigger, and the payoff hinges on the array being a genuine escape route that does not distort mid-transit, lest Shen’s primal fear of the Gu overrides any suspicion of the ruse.

I will approach the rival clan’s second elder, Ma Wei, under a blood oath of surrender, offering my cousin as a tribute to confirm my defection while I embed a scentless Soul-Leaf tracking Gu beneath her fingernail. I am betting that Ma Wei, who has long resented the clan leader’s monopoly on the Blood Skull refinement lore, will accept the tribute not to execute her immediately but to secretly study her unique physique for his own coup attempt. This hinges on Ma Wei’s documented ambition and his fear of being purged once the leader no longer needs his faction, making the acquisition of independent leverage his most probable and urgent priority.

I will perform the Soul-Binding Gu ritual with my cousin, intertwining our life forces so that her death grants me her full pure yin cultivation. I then broadcast this fact to the Mo clan's ambush commander, Elder Mo Xiu, betting that his fear of inadvertently creating an unstoppable foe will compel him to issue strict orders to capture, not kill, my cousin. This restraint is probable because Mo Xiu is a cautious tactician who would never risk triggering a power transfer that could instantly reverse the ambush's outcome, provided he believes the soul-link is genuine and not a bluff.

judge.note: Strategies 2-4 assume benefactor/Ma Wei/Mo Xiu will behave predictably without contingency for their deviation.

theme: rival wives and concubines maneuvering over an HEIR's INHERITANCE as the master weakens or dies
problem: Madam Lin, a concubine and mother of the merchant Qian’s only infant son, must act within the narrowing window of his final illness to secure the boy’s inheritance, knowing the childless head wife Madam Zheng and her magistrate brother will seize everything the moment Qian dies unless she outmaneuvers them now. The right choice hinges on anticipating how rivals react, servant factions realign, and the local magistrate’s venality can be redirected before the household collapses into a lethal scramble.
facets: The master’s mind is clouded by sickness; whoever is at his bedside in his lucid moments can obtain a decisive deathbed instruction or signature—but rivals watch constantly.
House servants are already dividing into factions, and key stewards can be bribed, threatened, or turned to spy on the concubines’ moves.
The magistrate’s favor is for sale, but his current patron is Madam Zheng’s brother, and any appeal to law risks exposing the household’s own bribery history.

angles.directive: Discredit or remove Madam Zheng before the master dies—plant evidence of an affair, poison her, or frame her for theft—so that her claim crumbles and the magistrate can be bought to recognize Lin’s son without opposition.

Spirits the son and a cache of portable wealth out of the household to a safe monastery or distant relative now, letting the master die while the boy is absent from the immediate danger; only reveal his survival and claim later when Zheng’s usurpation becomes obvious and public sentiment turns.

Propose a pact with Madam Zheng: she formally adopts the infant as her own son and legal heir, while Lin becomes her trusted ally; Zheng gets the public status and control of the household treasury during the boy’s minority, and Lin secures her child’s future and her own safety under Zheng’s protection.

Persuade the dying master to convert his wealth into a lineage trust managed by a neutral third party—a respected abbot or merchant guild elder—with the son as sole beneficiary at adulthood, thus nullifying both the head wife’s immediate control and the magistrate’s jurisdiction by making the inheritance a sacred, contractual matter outside household power plays.

threads: I will pay Steward Deng to plant a forged love letter from a junior clerk in Madam Zheng’s chambers, timed for discovery during a sudden inventory while the master’s mind is clear. I bet that Magistrate Heng will immediately abandon his sister and covertly endorse a will favoring Lin’s son to extinguish the scandal, because a public trial would invite inquiries into his court’s history of selling property judgments to this household. The bet hinges on Deng’s greed outweighing his fear of retaliation and the letter’s seal convincingly matching the clerk’

Madam Lin slips her infant son and a chest of silver out to Cloud Abode Monastery, while she stays bedside to deflect suspicion. I bet Madam Zheng will immediately cry kidnapping and demand her brother issue an arrest warrant, because a criminal accusation is her swiftest lever to seize the estate and extinguish the heir’s claim while Lin is isolated. The payoff hinges on Zheng’s subsequent misrule provoking the county’s merchant elders to formally petition the prefectual court, stripping her brother’s cover.

Lin approaches Madam Zheng directly and proposes a formal pact: Zheng legally adopts the infant as her own son and heir, gaining public status and unilateral control of the household treasury during the boy’s minority, while Lin is guaranteed permanent protection and a trusted subordinate role, surrendering all maternal claims in exchange for survival. This move bets that Madam Zheng will accept, because the offer feeds her core desire for legitimate matriarchal authority and a bloodless consolidation of power, sidelining her brother’s crude confiscation scheme. The bet hinges on Zheng’s vanity and hunger for social recognition outweighing her brother’s avarice, requiring that she calculates a stable, legally clean adoption delivers more lasting control than a violent, exposed scramble.

Lin will have the dying Qian sign a trust deed witnessed by the respected Abbot of the City God Temple, converting his entire estate into a lineage trust with the abbot as sole trustee and the infant son as beneficiary upon adulthood. She bets that Madam Zheng

judge.note: Strategy 4 incomplete; 1-3 assume unrealistic single-move capitulation chains.

theme: a newcomer concubine PROTECTING CONCEALED WEALTH from predatory rivals and the master himself
problem: You have smuggled a pouch of gold taels and a costly pearl hairpin into the household, but Second Wife has noticed the scent of your hidden wealth; the master watches your smallest luxury with suspicion, and Third Wife sees a pretext to brand you a thief. You must decide how to secure this treasure so that whichever faction springs the trap first, your future position survives.
facets: Second Wife moves by seeding rumors, not direct accusation—she can turn a servant's loose talk into a scandal without appearing involved.
The master values order over truth; if a theft is 'proven' by household consensus, he will punish to restore face, not to discover real guilt.
Third Wife covets social leverage more than gold—if she can claim you gifted her the hairpin under duress, she gains both ornament and victimhood.

angles.directive: Pre-emptively present a portion of the treasure to the master as a filial offering from your 'impoverished family,' weaving a public story that turns hidden wealth into visible loyalty and dares rivals to slander a virtuous act.

Secretly deposit the entire pouch with a trustworthy-outwardly-disinterested outsider—such as the old gatekeeper who owes you a silent debt—removing all physical evidence from your quarters so that any accusation crumbles upon search, deferring retrieval until the household's attention pivots.

Convert the gold into intangible assets by quietly forgiving the gambling debts of the master's wastrel nephew in exchange for a secret written favor, transmuting metal into future influence that cannot be found in a chest and reframing wealth as obligation.

Stage a controlled discovery where you 'catch' a low-ranking maid with the hairpin and a small silver piece, claiming she stole from you, eliminating the inevitable accusation by providing the household a guilty party and a narrative that satisfies the master's need for order before rivals can shape one.

threads: I will publicly present the pearl hairpin and a portion of the gold to the master as a filial offering from my impoverished family, framing this sacrifice as homage to his authority. This move bets that Third Wife, seeing the hairpin, will publicly claim it was stolen to seize both ornament and victimhood—her deepest incentive. The reaction depends on the master viewing her accusation as a disruption of filial order rather than proof of theft, requiring his instinct to preserve face through visible loyalty to override his suspicion.

I pass the full pouch to the old gatekeeper at dawn, reminding him of his son’s escape; he buries it among his firewood, incurious. I am betting that Second Wife, tipped that my rooms are bare, will plant a servant to swear they saw me conceal a bundle—her most probable move because rumor alone cannot satisfy the master’s demand for tangible proof of theft. The contingency is the gatekeeper’s nerve: if the master’s interrogation terrifies him more than his debt, he will reveal the cache and gift her the evidence she lacks.

I will approach the wastrel nephew, Third Uncle’s son who drowns in dice debts at the Drunken Lotus Hall, and purchase his signed promissory notes from the gambling house at a fraction of their face value, then forgive them entirely in a private meeting in exchange for his sworn written pledge to advocate for my household standing before the master should any accusation of theft arise—a document I will seal and cache outside the estate. I am betting that Second Wife, upon hearing a servant’s whisper that I visited the nephew, will interpret this as a clumsy romantic indiscretion rather than a financial maneuver, because her own rise to power depended entirely on exploiting sexual scandal and she reflexively frames every rival’s secret action through that lens. The bet pays only if the nephew’s reputation as a dissolute fool holds firm enough that Second Wife cannot imagine him as a credible political ally, causing her to waste her first strike on an adultery rumor that collapses under scrutiny and exhausts her servant’s credibility before she pivots to the real treasure.

I hide the hairpin and a sliver of silver in scullion Lotus’s bedding, then ‘catch’ her before the housekeeper. I bet Third Wife, watching the master flog Lotus without hesitation to seal the matter, will suppress her own accusation that I gifted the hairpin under duress—her victimhood act requires ordering the household’s narrative, not fracturing it. The wager rests on the master’s immediate, unreasoning punishment; if he questions the evidence, her hunger for leverage overpowers her caution.

judge.note: Strategies 3-4 spiral into implausible psychological reads; 2 depends on gatekeeper nerve.

theme: holding a newly conquered HEREDITARY state whose old ruling bloodline still has popular sympathy
problem: You have conquered a duchy where the young exiled heir of the deposed duke is beloved, and intelligence reveals a nascent conspiracy among local magnates to restore him—yet the plot’s breadth, timing, and foreign backing remain unknown. Your response must anticipate whether a preemptive purge will extinguish the threat or ignite a wider revolt, whether the rival kingdom sheltering the heir will seize the chance to invade, and whether your own locally recruited soldiers will stay loyal if the old bloodline is invoked.
facets: The conspiracy’s true extent is opaque: it may be a fringe of discontented nobles or a deep network awaiting a signal.
The exiled heir resides under the protection of an ambitious neighboring monarch, who could exploit any instability to press a dynastic claim.
Your occupying force partly relies on local militia whose ancestral allegiance to the old ruling house might be rekindled by a crisis.

angles.directive: Strike preemptively: arrest the suspected magnates publicly for treason, and dispatch covert agents to eliminate the exiled heir before he becomes a rallying point, betting that swift terror will paralyze further resistance.

Feign ignorance while quietly inserting informants, allowing the plot to ripen; delay any visible crackdown until the conspirators commit overt acts, then expose them with undeniable proof to justify a legally unassailable purge.

Offer the disaffected magnates prestigious offices and negotiate a marriage between your own kin and the exiled heir, bringing him back under your roof as a controlled vassal, thus merging the bloodlines and neutralizing the restoration motive.

Launch a propaganda campaign recasting the old duke as a cruel tyrant and yourself as a divinely favored liberator; institute new festivals, rewrite local chronicles, and redistribute symbolic honors to erode the populace’s sentimental attachment to the former dynasty.

threads: We will publicly arrest the suspected magnates in a treason dragnet while covert operatives eliminate the exiled heir in his foreign refuge, severing the conspiracy’s domestic leaders and its rallying figurehead in a single shock. I am betting that the neighboring monarch, suddenly deprived of his dynastic pawn and witnessing our ruthless speed, will calculate that a unilateral invasion now lacks a legitimate claimant to sway the duchy’s militia, making a cautious stand-down his likeliest course. This hinges entirely on the assassination remaining deniable and on that monarch’s ambition being disciplined by a cold aversion to a costly, figureless war.

I will appoint a clandestine corps of royal notaries and scribes, disguised as tax assessors, to systematically embed themselves within the households of every magnate suspected of disloyalty, instructing them to do nothing but observe and document until a written oath or a foreign-signed communiqué physically changes hands. This is a bet that Duke-Regent Aldric, the exiled heir’s uncle and the conspiracy’s most cautious internal broker, will interpret our regime’s apparent negligence as administrative incompetence rather than a trap, emboldening him to personally receive and cache the rival kingdom’s sealed promises of military support in his own study. His incentive is to control the physical proof of foreign backing to secure his post-restoration primacy over more reckless co-conspirators, and this is the likeliest reaction only if our informants’ civilian covers remain uncompromised by any overeager provincial commander.

By betrothing the exiled heir to my daughter and installing the three most vocal magnates as co-regents of a reconstituted vassal duchy, I collapse the conspiracy’s political rationale and bind the old bloodline to my dynasty’s survival. I am betting that the rival king sheltering him will read this merger as the imminent death of his casus belli and launch an immediate, spoiling invasion to shatter the settlement before the marriage is solemnized. This holds only if the heir can be extracted from that court and calculates that a subordinate throne is safer than lifelong exile as a foreign pawn.

I will launch a “Liberation Chronicle” campaign, dispatching royal heralds and commissioned playwrights to every market town to publicly recite fabricated accounts of the old duke’s secret grain hoarding and child conscription, while simultaneously inaugurating a “Festival of Unshackling” where I personally bestow heirloom signet rings—reforged from the melted ducal seal—upon local yeomen. This bets that the exiled heir’s foremost domestic champion, the Dowager Countess of Verlaine, will be provoked into a premature, isolated denunciation of the festival as sacrilege before her network is fully armed, because her identity is rooted in being the sole custodian of the old house’s sacred memory and she will perceive the ritual desecration as an existential assault requiring immediate public defense. The strategy hinges on the contingency that her emotional outrage overrides her strategic patience, leading her to act before her foreign backers have finalized their invasion logistics.

judge.note: Strategies 3 & 4 rely on implausibly precise emotional/strategic triggers.

theme: whether a dominant assembly should ANNIHILATE or spare a REVOLTED SUBJECT-CITY as a warning to other restless allies
problem: After a brutal siege, the Athenian assembly must decide whether to annihilate the Mytileneans for their revolt or rescind the death decree, anticipating how each choice shapes the loyalty of subject-allies and the calculations of Sparta.
facets: Annihilation could deter future revolts by terror, or harden resistance if cities see no mercy in surrender.
Sparing the city fractures the rebel faction between oligarchs (who instigated revolt) and the demos (who later surrendered), creating internal leverage.

Sparta watches closely — if Athens shows irresolution, Spartan-backed oligarchs in other cities will be emboldened; if Athens butchers the city, Sparta gains a propaganda victory for its 'liberator' narrative.

angles.directive: Execute the original decree: kill the adult male population, enslave the women and children, and raze the walls — betting that swift, horrific punishment will paralyze other restive allies with fear despite the moral cost.

Blame the revolt solely on the Mytilenean oligarchs, execute only them, and empower the pro-Athenian demos with land redistribution — transforming Mytilene into a dependent showcase of Athenian clemency that splits future rebel coalitions along class lines.

Hand the fate of Mytilene to a specially convened tribunal of allied cities — making them complicit in the verdict, deflecting sole responsibility from Athens, and testing which allies will counsel mercy before Sparta can exploit the moment.

Spare the city and declare the Athenian Empire a voluntary league reformed, inviting Mytilene to negotiate new terms of alliance instead of tribute — a radical rebranding that undercuts Sparta's 'liberation' casus belli and redefines Athenian power as consent-based before the wider Greek world.

threads: The Assembly must dispatch a swift trireme to countermand yesterday’s reprieve and execute the original decree: kill every Mytilenean man, enslave the women and children, raze the walls. This move bets that the Spartan-backed oligarchs in restive cities like Chios, witnessing total annihilation, will calculate that no Spartan fleet can arrive before Athenian retribution obliterates them too, and thus abandon conspiracy. The bet hinges on Athens’ naval supremacy making the threat of swift, repeatable extermination outweigh Sparta’s liberator narrative.

Athens will execute only the oligarchic instigators, redistribute their land to the Mytilenean demos, and install a garrison as guarantor of the new class order. The bet: Spartan-backed oligarchs in restive allies like Chios, seeing Mytilene’s demos enriched at their leaders’ expense, will most likely recoil from revolt, fearing their own commons now have a material incentive to betray them to Athens. Mytilene’s demos must resist Spartan subversion; if they defect, the class-splitting model unravels, and oligarchs elsewhere return to betting on Spartan rescue.

Athens will empanel a tribunal of allied delegates and bind itself to their verdict on Mytilene’s fate. The bet is that the delegates, calculating that a vote for annihilation establishes a precedent that could later justify their own cities’ destruction, will counsel mercy. The likeliest reaction is a clemency vote, making the allies complicit and denying Sparta a clean propaganda coup; this hinges on the allies’ trust that Athens will not later punish those who voted to spare the city.

Athens announces it will spare Mytilene and renegotiate its membership as a voluntary ally in a reformed league, abolishing tribute. The strategy wagers that the Mytilenean demos, fearing retribution from the return of the oligarchs they betrayed, will preemptively hand them over to Athens in exchange for guaranteed local autonomy. This only holds if the demos trusts the offer’s sincerity before Spartan emissaries persuade them it is a trap to isolate and destroy them.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 assume demos loyalty; fragile. Strategy 3 assumes ally rationality.

theme: a small NEUTRAL city pressured to SUBMIT to a stronger power or stake survival on justice and distant allies
problem: As the neutral city of Melos, you must decide whether to submit to Athenian demands for tribute and alliance or refuse, trusting in Spartan protection and the justice of your cause. The right choice hinges on whether Sparta will actually sail to your aid before Athens starves you out, and whether any Athenian setback elsewhere will weaken their resolve here.
facets: Spartan aid is constrained by their annual festival cycle and the need to keep forces near the Peloponnese for helot revolts.
Athenian siege capability is immense but expensive; a protracted resistance could drain their treasury and embolden other neutrals.
Submission preserves the population immediately but erodes Melian autonomy and makes you a permanent Athenian dependency.

angles.directive: Refuse submission and fortify for a prolonged siege, gambling that Sparta will eventually send a relief fleet and that Athenian costs and casualties will fracture their domestic support for the campaign.

Request a formal truce to send an embassy to Sparta and the other Peloponnesian allies, using the diplomatic delay to shift the immediate pressure onto Sparta to declare intent, while buying time for weather or other conflicts to divert Athenian attention.

Open negotiations for a conditional alliance that preserves internal self-rule and commits Melian ships to the Athenian fleet, positioning Melos as a valuable junior partner rather than a crushed subject, and creating a faction within Athens that sees your survival as useful.

Publicly broadcast the Athenian ultimatum to all Greek cities, framing the choice as a test of Hellenic law and Spartan credibility, thereby transforming a bilateral power play into a reputational crisis that forces Sparta to act or lose all moral authority with its allies.

threads: Melos will refuse tribute, stockpile grain and water for eighteen months, and dispatch envoys to Sparta timed to arrive as the Karneia festival ends, explicitly invoking Sparta’s oath to protect free Hellenes. This move bets that the Spartan ephors, fearing that Melos’ fall would trigger a cascade of neutral defections to Athens, will dispatch a relief squadron under a trusted navarch like Alcidas once ritual obligations permit. The bet’s payoff hinges on the helot population remaining quiescent through the summer, freeing the Spartans to risk a naval expedition without inviting a revolt in their rear.

Melos requests a truce from Athenian commanders Cleomedes and Tisias to send envoys directly to the Spartan ephors, demanding a public declaration of intervention. The bet is that the ephors, pressed by allied observers and terrified of losing credibility, will most likely respond with a conditional but solemn pledge, promising aid after the Karneia festival if helot security permits. This only works if the Athenian assembly, seeing Sparta’s ambiguous signal and the truce’s diplomatic cover, decides delay is cheaper than an immediate, costly siege.

Melos dispatches envoys proposing immediate integration as a self-governing naval ally, contributing ten triremes and port access in return for non-interference in domestic law. This move bets that Alcibiades, calculating the Sicilian expedition’s need for every available hull and the delay a siege would impose, will become the most vocal Athenian advocate for acceptance—he stands to gain a ready force increment and a propaganda victory of voluntary accession. The bet’s payoff hinges on the expedition’s departure timetable: if the fleet is already fully manned and departure imminent, the assembly’s hardliners can frame Melos’s last-minute offer as a cynical ploy and a siege as the necessary deterrent.

Melos dispatches heralds to every major polis to publicly recite the Athenian ultimatum verbatim, explicitly framing submission as a verdict on Spartan credibility and Hellenic nomos. This bets that the Spartan ephors, confronting a wave of allied outrage and the imminent risk of Corinth recalculating its security under a protector who abandons a neutral, will most likely override the sacred calendar and dispatch a relief squadron to silence the charge of hypocrisy. The move’s payoff hinges entirely on whether the heralds can broadcast the message before Athens interdicts the sea lanes to isolate the crisis from its intended audience.

judge.note: Strategies 1,3,4 rely on contingencies (helot quiescence, Alcibiades' priorities, herald speed) that feel under-grounded historically.

theme: an oligarchic faction's bid to OVERTHROW a democracy by promising stability, money, and a foreign patron
problem: As leader of the oligarchic club in a wavering democracy, you must decide whether to accept Persian gold now to fund a coup — knowing the Great King expects installed clients to cede Ionian cities, which will enrage your own hoplites and invite immediate Athenian intervention.
facets: The Persian satrap's gold arrives with a contract: immediate payment against a signed, sealed promise to restore Persian sovereignty over three coastal cities after the coup succeeds.
The current democratic assembly is deadlocked and bankrupt, but the fleet's rowers — the democratic base — are away on campaign; they will return in 30 days, furious and armed.
A Spartan harmost with a small garrison sits two days' march away, watching the situation; he has orders to stabilize the region but no clear directive on which faction to back.

angles.directive: Take the Persian gold publicly into the city treasury as an emergency loan from a foreign benefactor, credit the oligarchs with saving the city from default, and use the gratitude to win the coming election before the fleet returns — then renegotiate the Ionian clause from a position of legitimate authority.

Accept the gold secretly and strike now before the fleet returns: seize the acropolis at night, execute the democratic leaders, and present the Spartans with a sealed alliance and a fait accompli — daring them to choose between a stable oligarchic ally and a chaotic democratic mob.

Use the Persian offer as proof of a foreign conspiracy to enslave the city, leak it to the democratic factions yourself, and ride the patriotic backlash to purge both extreme oligarchs and corrupt demagogues — forging a new 'emergency constitution' that concentrates power in a property-owning middle ground, with you as its architect.

Stall the Persian envoy while sending a trusted emissary to the returning fleet's commander — your cousin — offering to share power if the fleet anchors offshore and 'arbitrates' the crisis; let the rowers' anger be aimed at the most extreme oligarchs you have already secretly marked for sacrifice, preserving yourself as the indispensable moderate.

threads: I will deposit the Persian gold into the public treasury as an “Emergency Civic Loan from a Philhellene Benefactor,” immediately funding the overdue garrison wages and grain dole to credit the oligarchic club with solvency before the assembly can call a snap election. This move bets that the democratic leader, Cleon, will respond by accelerating the fleet’s recall rather than waiting the full thirty days, because the sudden fiscal rescue neutralizes his only campaign weapon—the oligarchs’ supposed bankruptcy—and forces him to gamble on a premature armed confrontation before we consolidate legal authority. The contingency is that the Spartan harmost remains strictly neutral during this window of democratic absence, interpreting his stabilization orders narrowly rather than marching on the city to exploit the temporary power vacuum.

I will accept the Persian gold, seize the acropolis in a night assault, and execute the democratic leaders before dawn, then present the Spartan harmost with a sealed alliance treaty and the accomplished fact of an oligarchic government. I am betting that the harmost, lacking clear orders and fearing the imminent return of an armed democratic mob, will judge a stable oligarchic client the safer bet and commit his garrison to defend us. The wager hinges entirely on the coup appearing instantly decisive and orderly, not a bloody turmoil that would compel him to intervene to restore order.

I will leak the satrap’s sealed contract—not to the assembly, but directly to the trierarchs of the absent fleet through a trusted intermediary, framing it as proof that the extreme oligarchs intend to trade Ionian freedom for Persian backing. This bets that the harmost, Cleandridas, will interpret the ensuing patriotic fury as a mandate to back my middle-ground coup rather than risk a democratic mob sacking the Spartan garrison; his overriding incentive is to stabilize the region without committing Spartan hoplites to a street fight he cannot win, making him the most likely external actor to ratify a fait accompli. The contingency is that the fleet rowers must intercept the news and return within the 30-day window before the oligarchs can purge the intermediary and discredit the document.

I stall the satrap’s envoy with feasts while my freedman reaches my cousin’s flagship, offering co-rule if he anchors offshore and channels the rowers’ rage against a list of oligarchs I have secretly condemned as Persian tools. I bet my cousin seizes this offer, because it grants him immediate power and eliminates factional rivals without the chaos of a blind purge that could endanger his own officers. This rests entirely on the rowers never learning of my own secret overture to Persia before the condemned oligarchs are dead.

judge.note: Strategies 3–4 rely on implausibly tight timing windows and information control.

theme: FORTRESSES vs the goodwill of the people as the prince's true defense
problem: A prince who has just annexed a fiercely independent city-state must decide whether to build a fortress to overawe the populace or to disarm and cultivate their goodwill, all while rival princes circle and exiled nobles plot their return.
facets: The city's deep-rooted republican pride and resentment of foreign rule
The ambition and military readiness of neighboring powers to exploit unrest
The shifting loyalty of the prince's own mercenary captains, who may negotiate with the exiled nobles

angles.directive: Build an imposing fortress, disarm the populace, and execute any suspected dissidents to crush the city's spirit before foreign rivals can exploit it.
Raze any existing fortifications, grant tax exemptions, and marry into a distinguished local family, converting the city's elite into stakeholders of your rule.
Feign a withdrawal to appease the populace while secretly funding rival factions within the city, letting internal discord consume their rebellious energy until they beg for your protection.

Declare the city your new capital, launch a grand cultural renaissance that blends your heritage with theirs, and institute a novel civic council that gives voice to the people under your ultimate authority, redefining their identity as the heart of your principality.

threads: I will immediately construct a fortress on the high ground overlooking the city’s central forum—garrisoned by my most reliable household troops, not mercenaries—and simultaneously decree a total disarmament of the citizenry, followed by the public execution of the ten most vocal republican orators identified by my intelligence network. This move bets that Duke Aldric of neighboring Veridia, who is already marshaling his forces, will choose to accelerate his covert funding of the exiled nobles rather than launch an open invasion. The likeliest driver of his reaction is Aldric’s deep aversion to risking his own standing army in a direct assault on prepared fortifications when he can instead bleed my treasury and distract my garrison through a protracted proxy insurgency. The entire bet hinges on the assumption that my household troops’ loyalty holds firm and is not itself corroded by the very brutality they are ordered to enact.

I will raze the old city walls and exempt the leading merchant houses from the prince’s tribute for a generation, then seal the arrangement by marrying the daughter of the city’s most revered former magistrate. This move bets that Lord Aldric, the exiled noble commanding the largest mercenary company across the border, will immediately pivot from plotting invasion to demanding a negotiated buy-in, because the tax exemption and marriage create a rival bloc of local elites whose vested interest in the new order would collapse if Aldric’s unpaid captains sacked their counting houses. The bet hinges entirely on the mercenary captains valuing a guaranteed, bloodless share of the city’s restored trade revenues over the uncertain plunder Aldric has been promising.

The prince announces a staged withdrawal of his garrison while covertly channeling funds through a neutral merchant house to arm both the Artisans’ League and the Dockers’ Brotherhood, each convinced they are his sole proxy. This bets that exiled Lord Verazzo will immediately sail back to seize control of the Dockers, driven by his fear that the League will otherwise monopolize the grain supply and by his family’s deep dockland loyalties. The move hinges on mercenary captain Voss remaining bought; if Voss defects to Verazzo, the factions will instead unite against a common foreign puppet.

By relocating his court to the annexed city and declaring it the new capital, the prince reframes occupation as elevation, immediately commissioning a hybrid architectural style that physically melds his ancestral motifs with their republican iconography. He simultaneously installs a “People’s Tribune” council with public debate rights and a binding budget veto, while reserving all military and foreign-policy appointments for himself. This entire consolidation bets that the neighboring Duke of Verulia, deprived of the exiled nobles’ narrative that the city groans under a foreign heel, will likely pause his invasion preparations rather than risk a costly assault on a fortressless but genuinely unified capital. The bet hinges on the Duke calculating that his own mercenary captains will not honor contracts to fight a populace defending its own seat of government—a calculation that holds only so long as the new civic council visibly exercises its veto and the populace does not perceive the cultural program as a cosmetic veneer.

judge.note: Strategy 3's dual-proxy gambit assumes Voss loyalty too casually; Strategy 4 underestimates cosmetic-veneer risk.

theme: a DOPPELGANGER has replaced a cherished relative: expose the spy at the cost of the real kin
problem: Your beloved younger sister, whose talent for gu refinement is the clan's future deterrent, has been replaced by a faceless assassin using a legendary transformation Gu—you've identified the imposter, but the original's soul is sealed inside the assassin's Gu worm, and killing the doppelganger will shatter the Gu and destroy her soul; the clan elder council is three days from naming her successor to the Ancestral Gu vault, giving the spy access to clan-defining secrets. Do you expose her before then and lose your sister forever, or let the ceremony proceed and gamble on a window to capture both the spy and the Gu intact?
facets: The doppelganger's information network extends beyond the clan—it feeds a rival elder faction's strategist who will accelerate their coup timeline the instant exposure occurs, while paradoxically also being the only one who knows how to reverse the soul-sealing Gu without destroying it.

The real sister's talent is the clan's negotiating leverage in an imminent three-sect alliance summit; if she's publicly revealed as compromised or dead, the clan's bargaining position collapses, potentially triggering a resource war that would kill thousands.

The transference ceremony involves attuning the Ancestral Gu to the inheritor's bloodline resonance—if the spy completes even half the ritual, the Gu will imprint on the doppelganger's disguised aura, corrupting the clan's foundational treasure and birthing a sentient calamity Gu.

angles.directive: Approach the doppelganger privately and offer a binding soul contract: you will help it complete the ceremony and seize the Ancestral Gu—provided it releases your sister and defects to you alone, betraying its original faction and installing you as the power behind the corrupted Gu's new wielder, all while you secretly prepare a counter-ritual to devour both Gu and spy at the final resonance moment.

Anonymously leak to the rival faction's strategist that 'their operative is planning a double-cross at the ceremony,' feeding false evidence of the spy's impending betrayal so the rivals panic and attempt to remotely sever the soul-sealing Gu's link—creating a chaotic window during which you can intercept the severing backlash, extract your sister's soul into a prepared receptacle, and let both factions destroy each other.

Instead of treating the ceremony as a trap, accelerate a hidden bloodline corruption project you'd abandoned: deliberately let the spy complete the imprinting, but use a Heaven's Will fragment you've cultivated to overwrite the nascent calamity Gu's consciousness with a copy of your sister's soul-fragment (salvaged from a childhood Gu accident), effectively 'reincarnating' her as the Gu spirit while the spy becomes its first meal—transforming clan, sister, and weapon into a single ascended entity.

Confront the spy silently with ironclad proof but make no move—allow the ceremony, endure the Ancestral Gu's corruption into a calamity, and let the clan's destruction play out naturally; then, in the aftermath when the calamity Gu devours the doppelganger and gains sentience, present yourself as the one who 'preserved' the clan's memory, offering the newborn Gu-entity a familiar identity and purpose as your sister reborn, binding it through emotional imprint rather than force.

threads: I will approach the doppelganger with a Heaven’s Oath contract offering my complicity in the attunement ritual in exchange for the assassin’s true name and the unsealing of my sister’s soul into a pre-prepared spirit-trapping jar, positioning myself as the sole power behind its corrupted succession. I am betting that the rival strategist, Advisor Cai, will immediately escalate the coup timeline upon detecting the doppelganger’s unauthorized defection, because her faction’s leverage depends entirely on controlling the spy’s access to the vault and she cannot tolerate a rogue asset wielding the Ancestral Gu independently. This hinges on the doppelganger’s rational self-preservation overriding its implanted loyalty; it must calculate that serving me offers a higher survival probability than returning to Cai empty-handed.

I anonymously feed forged evidence to rival strategist Joren Ashveil proving his doppelganger operative intends to seize the Ancestral Gu during the ceremony, seeding falsified communication trails. I bet Joren’s consuming terror of losing control over the clan’s foundational treasure will trigger an immediate remote activation of the soul-sealing Gu’s emergency severance protocol, destroying the asset before she can betray him. The window opens only if Joren possesses a long-range severance trigger and his reflexive paranoia overrides his verification habits—if he pauses to authenticate, the severance backlash never materializes.

I will let the spy complete half the bloodline attunement, then inject Heaven’s Will and my sister’s salvaged soul-fragment to overwrite the nascent Gu, reincarnating her as its spirit while it devours the spy. This bets that Strategist Wei, mistaking the resulting bloodline-tainted Gu for successful corruption, will immediately launch his coup by mobilizing the spy’s entire network to seize the ancestral vault. Contingency: his ritual monitoring must be aura-only and miss the soul insertion; if he spots the overwrite, he retreats silently, network intact.

I confront the assassin-doppelganger with irrefutable proof, then withdraw entirely, letting the ceremony corrupt the Ancestral Gu into a sentient calamity that devours her. I bet the newborn Gu-entity, its consciousness a

judge.note: Strategies 2-4 hallucinate implausible cascades; lack grounding.

theme: whether to honor a costly DEFENSIVE ALLIANCE with a quarrelsome partner that risks dragging the state into a wider war
problem: You are a leading citizen of Phlius, a small Peloponnesian polis bound by a defensive alliance to quarrelsome Cleonae, a neighbor that has provoked Argos with repeated raids. Argos now threatens war unless Cleonae submits, and Cleonae invokes your pact. Honoring the alliance means risking a wider war that could draw in Sparta and Athens; dishonoring it preserves immediate peace but destroys your city's credibility and leaves you exposed to future predation. The right choice depends on anticipating how each power will move in the coming months.
facets: Sparta's protection of Phlius is uncertain—it might welcome a proxy fight with Argos or abandon you to avoid escalation.
Athens may seize the chance to ally with Argos against Spartan interests, turning a local quarrel into a broader Peloponnesian struggle.
Cleonae's own survival is doubtful; a full commitment could mean sacrifice for a partner that collapses, while a swift show of force might deter Argos entirely.

angles.directive: Launch a joint preemptive strike with Cleonae against Argos' muster, aiming to shatter their army in a single blow and force a quick settlement before external powers can intervene.
Stall and send only token 'observers' while urging Cleonae to open negotiations, buying months to see whether Sparta will impose a truce or Argos's resolve will weaken under the cost of waiting.

Propose absorbing Cleonae and other small poleis into a new Phliasian-led League of Mutual Protection, transforming the alliance from a bilateral hazard into a collective security pact that can deter Argos with combined weight.

Renounce the bilateral pact and declare Phlius a sacred neutral ground under the protection of Zeus Eleutherios, inviting Sparta, Argos, and Athens to guarantee your borders and reframing the crisis as a Panhellenic test of honoring oaths.

threads: Phlius must commit its entire hoplite levy to a dawn assault alongside Cleonae on the Argive muster at the Thyrea plain, driving into their half-formed phalanx to annihilate it before noon. This bets Sparta’s ephors, dreading a proxy war but coveting a crippled Argos, will accept the fait accompli and withhold their army, rather than escalate to punish an outcome that spares them the very escalation they fear. The contingency: the strike must so thoroughly shatter the Argive phalanx in hours that no Athenian hawk or Spartan war party can exploit the crisis.

Phlius will dispatch a single unarmed delegation to Cleonae as “observers” while publicly petitioning Sparta to arbitrate and urging Cleonae to suspend raids and open talks. We bet that Argos’s war council, perceiving our non-commitment and the diplomatic off-ramp, will pause its mobilization rather than risk forcing Sparta’s hand by attacking a Spartan ally’s ally. This hinges on Sparta promptly offering to mediate—if it hesitates, Argos reads our stall as abandonment and strikes within the month.

Phlius must immediately propose a Phliasian-led League of Mutual Protection, absorbing Cleonae and any willing neighboring polis into a unified defensive pact with pooled hoplite levies under a rotating Phliasian strategos. This move bets that Argos, calculating that assaulting a consolidated multi-polis front would be disproportionately costly and risk drawing Sparta into a stabilizing intervention, will most likely suspend its ultimatum and seek a negotiated truce. The strategy hinges on the league's instant credibility: if any member hesitates to commit forces, Argos will perceive a hollow bluff and strike before coordination solidifies.

Phlius renounces the defensive pact with Cleonae and consecrates itself as sacred neutral ground under Zeus Eleutherios, formally inviting Sparta, Argos, and Athens to guarantee its borders. This move bets that Argos, confronted with a Panhellenic religious shield, will halt its threats rather than risk an impiety accusation that Sparta would eagerly weaponize to rally neutral cities. The contingency is Sparta’s swift, public embrace of the guarantor role—without that credible deterrent, Argos dismisses the consecration as a transparent bluff.

judge.note: Strategy 1 assumes Sparta's calculus too neatly; Strategy 4 over-relies on religious credibility.

theme: recalling a brilliant but DANGEROUS COMMANDER for trial mid-campaign vs leaving him in the field where he is winning
problem: As the chief strategos of your maritime alliance, you possess credible intelligence that your most brilliant admiral, commanding the fleet that holds back enemy incursions, is negotiating to switch sides in exchange for a personal principality. Recalling him for treason risks him leading the fleet into the enemy’s arms, devastating your naval power; leaving him risks him completing his defection and attacking your home waters with insider knowledge. How do you move to preserve your city’s hegemony?
facets: The admiral's officers are bound to him by years of shared victory and resentment against the home assembly’s interference in military affairs.
The enemy has signaled they would welcome him as a satrap, granting resources and autonomy, making defection a highly attractive path.
The assembly is deeply factionalized; a public trial could erupt into civil violence, as the admiral’s political allies might seize the citadel rather than see him condemned.

angles.directive: Dispatch a trusted marine with a small, loyal crew to board his flagship on the pretense of delivering a laurel crown, then kill him in his cabin, blame the enemy, and rally the fleet behind his second-in-command.

Send a false message that a plague has broken out in the fleet’s home port, requiring his immediate return with half the ships to protect the city’s grain supply; once he docks, isolate his vessel and arrest him discreetly, while the remaining ships hold the line.

Publicly decree him the title of ‘Liberator of the Seas’ and offer him the hand of your own daughter, with promises of a joint archonship upon his triumphant return; send envoys to negotiate a grand homecoming, aiming to envelop him in honors and slowly dilute his independent command.

Leak forged letters to his camp suggesting the enemy intends to murder him and seize his fleet once they rendezvous; circulate rumors among his crews that he is being used as bait, so that his own paranoia turns him back into a fierce patriot needing the city’s backing to crush the plot.

threads: I deploy a handpicked marine squad to deliver a laurel crown, murder the admiral in his cabin, plant forged enemy sigils, then declare the killing an enemy act and rally the fleet to his deputy, Klearchos. I bet Klearchos, once in sole command, will channel the fleet’s fury into an immediate retaliatory strike, because his fragile new authority depends entirely on the enemy-blame narrative holding, and any hesitation risks his own officers questioning the story. This hinges on the squad eliminating all witnesses and on Klearchos’ ambition overwhelming any residual loyalty to the dead admiral’s defection plans.

I will dispatch a sealed courier with a forged emergency decree, bearing the assembly’s seal, commanding the admiral to detach exactly half his fleet and return to port under the pretense of a quarantine cordon protecting the grain reserves. This move bets that his senior squadron commander, a man publicly slighted by the admiral during the last prize-money distribution, will enforce the separation order himself. His probable compliance hinges on a cultivated resentment and the prospect of immediate battlefield command, but the entire gambit collapses if the admiral’s personal intelligence network intercepts the real health reports from the capital before the false courier arrives.

I will issue a public decree naming him *Liberator of the Seas* and betrothing him to my daughter, while simultaneously dispatching envoys to negotiate a joint archonship upon his return, a move that bets on his veteran officers interpreting the honors not as a trap but as a legitimate path to shared power. The most probable reaction is that his chief lieutenant, a man known to resent the admiral’s personal ambition over the fleet’s collective glory, will pressure him to accept, fearing that a defection to serve as a foreign satrap would shatter the command unity built on their shared resentment of the home assembly. This hinges entirely on the admiral’s inner circle valuing their corporate identity as the fleet’s true guardians over the raw material rewards of enemy service.

We plant forged enemy dispatches—discovered by a trusted merchant captain we turn—detailing a plan to lure the admiral into a ceremonial rendezvous, then assassinate him and absorb his fleet under a rival claimant, Admiral Thessalos, whom the enemy is secretly grooming as satrap. This is a bet that the admiral’s officers, steeped in years of shared suspicion toward outsiders and personal loyalty to him, will read the forgery as confirmation that the enemy sees him as disposable, triggering their instinct to close ranks and urge him to break off negotiations preemptively. The probable reaction is that the admiral, cornered between a home assembly that loathes him and a foreign patron that now appears to be plotting his murder, recalculates survival by publicly recommitting to the city—framing his prior signals as a deception operation to expose enemy treachery—because his only remaining path to personal security is to make himself indispensable to the fleet’s morale. This hinges entirely on the contingency that Thessalos is a credible enough figure in the enemy’s court that the forgery’s named threat feels plausible to officers who already fear being sidelined by foreign intrigues.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 rely on implausibly perfect information control; witness elimination fantasy in 1.

theme: launching a distant, glittering FOREIGN EXPEDITION that overextends the state vs consolidating strength at home
problem: As Syracuse pleads for aid against Athens, you must decide whether to dispatch the fleet to Sicily to secure new tribute and deny Spartan allies a western grain supply, or conserve your strained manpower at home where a Spartan invasion force gathers on your border.
facets: Athenian treasury is diminished from plague and prior campaigns; the fleet's absence risks leaving the Piraeus undefended if Sparta moves by sea.
Syracuse is a democracy with a large population and deep harbors—if Athens wins, other neutral Sicilian cities may swing alliance; if it fails, Sparta gains a propaganda victory.
Alcibiades, the expedition's champion, faces political enemies at home who might exploit any delay or defeat to bring down the war party and sue for peace.

angles.directive: Launch the full expedition immediately and seek a decisive naval battle before Syracuse can complete its harbor defenses, aiming to shatter their fleet and install a blockade that forces surrender within one season.

Send a token squadron to Segesta to satisfy treaty obligations but avoid engagement, while feigning preparation at home so Sparta commits to a pitched battle on territory of your choosing, delaying the western campaign until the home front is secured.

Dispatch envoys to negotiate a pan-Sicilian alliance against Carthaginian influence, offering Syracuse shared command of the expedition in exchange for tribute and a mutual defense pact, transforming their resistance into a joint venture.

Recall the fleet to fortify Attica's land borders and accept the loss of western ambitions, letting Syracuse fall to its own internal factions while conserving every ship to exhaust Spartan resolve through a protracted defensive war of attrition.

threads: We dispatch the full Athenian fleet under Nicias, Lamachus, and Alcibiades to intercept the Syracusan navy outside the Great Harbor before their defensive chain and palisades are complete, forcing a pitched battle on open water where our superior trireme maneuverability can shatter their inexperienced squadrons in a single afternoon. This bet hinges on the Spartan commander Gylippus interpreting our sudden concentration not as a gambit to be countered with his still-embryonic Corinthian reinforcement flotilla, but as a provocation he must avoid until his hoplite levies are fully mustered, because his personal prestige and the Spartan reputation for invincible land warfare will counsel against risking a naval defeat that would demoralize his Sicilian allies before a single phalanx marches. The contingency is that Syracuse’s democratic assembly, fearing the psychological shock of a defeat within sight of their walls, overrules the oligarchic war faction and withholds the authorization Gylippus needs to commit their triremes, granting us the decisive engagement before the harbor defenses seal.

Dispatch a token trireme squadron to Segesta solely to fulfill treaty obligations, while conspicuously massing hoplites and erecting fieldworks along the Acharnae road to feign a landward commitment. This bets that King Agis, reading the display as Athenian desperation after the plague, will commit his invasion force to a decisive set-piece battle on that prepared ground rather than revert to cautious ravaging. The reaction is probable because Spartan phalanx confidence and internal pressure to avenge Pylos outweigh caution, provided the feint deceives and the main fleet stays concealed at Piraeus.

We dispatch Lamachus—not Alcibiades or Nicias—to Syracuse with a joint command offer that names Carthage, not Sparta, as the shared threat, betting that Hermocrates, the Syracusan general who has long warned of Carthaginian ambition, will persuade his assembly to accept shared authority over a combined fleet rather than risk a two-front war he cannot win alone. This hinges on Hermocrates’ genuine fear of Carthage outweighing his suspicion of Athenian motives—a probable reaction given his documented advocacy, but one that collapses if the Syracusan democratic factions see the offer as a ploy to divide them before the Athenian fleet arrives in force.

We order the full fleet to disembark its marines to garrison the Long Walls and Attic forts, explicitly aborting the Sicilian expedition. This bets that King Agis, seeing no Athenian fleet to contest him at sea but also no hoplite army offering battle, will pillage the countryside briefly and then withdraw before winter, unwilling to storm fortified lines. Agis is most likely to recoil from a direct assault because Sparta’s war party fears catastrophic hoplite losses in siege warfare and the ephors will recall any king who bleeds manpower without extracting tribute. The entire play hinges on our crews holding Piraeus and the walls intact, denying Sparta even a hint of a naval breach.

judge.note: Strategies hallucinate implausible opponent psychology; chains exceed historical plausibility.
